Patch Set 1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

In osc_page_submit() it would be better to avoid adding "cli" on the stack for a single use, since Lustre is always stack constrained. 

Please also add the new OBD_BRW_SOFT_SYNC flag to the wirecheck and wiretest files.

I recall that there is some part of the code which checks whether there are different flags on adjacent pages and decides whether they can be merged or not. Something like osc_can_merge_pages(), but I can't check right now - plane is about to leave...

Patch Set 10: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(1 inline comment)

Oleg and I will be further discussing the heuristics for turning SOFT_SYNC into transaction commits next week in Beijing.

It might be expedient to separate out the client-side setting of OBD_BRW_SOFT_SYNC (which I don't think is contentious at all) from the server-side handling of this flag (which I think still needs more discussion).

Per Brian's comments in the bug:

 This soft sync can be handled asynchronously and the
 servers may optionally ignore it if they are already in
 the process of a sync. The ability to ignore the sync is
 particularly import for the ZFS OSDs to avoid thrashing.

and my comments inline, I think this part of the functional requirements have not yet been met.

What would be useful is to measure the performance of this patch with two different concurrent workloads.  A many-client IOR running by itself, a single-client IOR running by itself (should trigger this SOFT_SYNC code and is presumably faster ), and then the two of them running together to see the performance impact of SOFT_SYNC.  Ideally the many-client IOR would not be too negatively impacted by the SOFT_SYNC calls.

Patch Set 10:

Andreas, I did some initial testing of this patch on Grove using a 128 node IOR and a 8 node IOR. I configured the clients such that the 128 node clients would not bump against the unstable max (i.e. wont send soft syncs) but the 8 node clients would.

Specifically the tests I ran were:
    1. 128 node, 16 tasks per node, 64GB max_unstable_mb
    2. 8 node, 16 tasks per node, 1GB max_unstable_mb
    3. Concurrently ran two jobs:
         a. 128 node, 16 tasks per node, 64GB max_unstable_mb
         b. 8 node, 16 tasks per node, 1GB max_unstable_mb

What I saw was the large job was not affected by the smaller one sending soft syncs when the two were run concurrently. Although, the smaller job was negatively affected by the larger one. 

Here are the IOR results:

    Test 1:
    access    bw(MiB/s)  block(KiB) xfer(KiB)  open(s)    wr/rd(s)   close(s)   total(s)   iter
    ------    ---------  ---------- ---------  --------   --------   --------   --------   ----
    write     226753     8388608    1024.00    0.763591   73.06      0.165964   73.99      0
    write     220143     8388608    1024.00    0.849277   75.20      0.163224   76.21      1
    write     226348     8388608    1024.00    0.944016   72.98      0.198731   74.12      2
    write     226097     8388608    1024.00    0.892572   73.15      0.161255   74.20      3
    write     224460     8388608    1024.00    0.839469   73.74      0.166349   74.74      4
                                                                                
    Summary:                                                                    
    Operation   Max(MiB)   Min(MiB)  Mean(MiB)     StdDev    Mean(s)            
    write      226752.90  220143.02  224760.23    2436.69   74.65384

    Test 2:
    access    bw(MiB/s)  block(KiB) xfer(KiB)  open(s)    wr/rd(s)   close(s)   total(s)   iter
    ------    ---------  ---------- ---------  --------   --------   --------   --------   ----

    write     25560      8388608    1024.00    0.030867   40.98      0.008984   41.02      1
    write     23789      8388608    1024.00    0.028366   44.04      0.008914   44.08      2
    write     25486      8388608    1024.00    0.029760   41.11      0.009316   41.14      3
    write     25586      8388608    1024.00    0.030895   40.94      0.010273   40.98      4

    access    bw(MiB/s)  block(KiB) xfer(KiB)  open(s)    wr/rd(s)   close(s)   total(s)   iter

    write       24740.63    6649.60   13162.50    6759.39  100.34891

Note: file per process, 1 stripe per file, 128 OSS's used, 1 ZFS OST per OSS.

Patch Set 10: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Given the severity of this problem for single client workloads, I'm changing my inspection in light of the recent testing.

However, the issues pointed out in the previous inspection should still be addressed. In particular, moving the sync start to be after the current RPC is processed instead of before it would be a good idea.

parents.subject: LU-2139 osc: Track number of "unstable" pages per osc

commit.subject: LU-2139 osc: Use SOFT_SYNC to urge server commit
commit.message:
LU-2139 osc: Use SOFT_SYNC to urge server commit

This change adds a BRW page flag, OBD_BRW_SOFT_SYNC. This flag is
intended to urge a server to commit a client's unstable pages to
stable storage. A client will add this flag to any BRW requests while
it is in a state where it has "many" unstable pages pinned in its cache.

The server, upon receiving a page with this flag set, *should* begin
an async commit. The idea being that, with the proactive async commit,
the client's pinned unstable pages will transition into a stable state
faster than they would have otherwise. Although, the server side portion
of this agreement is still unimplemented, so the OBD_BRW_SOFT_SYNC flag
will currently fall on deaf ears.

Signed-off-by: Prakash Surya <surya1@llnl.gov>
Change-Id: I08946a5ec6afab390655643f6d3c8320bf010aea
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/4375
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Jinshan Xiong <jinshan.xiong@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
